title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Sam Foster, who presents a community radio programme.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today we're talking to Sam Foster, who presents a community radio programme. Sam, you present a programme on community radio. How did you first get involved? || Guest: I used to listen to the station while cooking, but I never imagined speaking on it. One day they asked for volunteers to help at a local music day. [20]I started by carrying equipment at an outdoor event, and the presenters noticed I was good at talking to visitors[/20]. A few weeks later they invited me to read short announcements, and slowly I became more confident behind the microphone. || Interviewer: What was difficult when you were starting out? || Guest: The hardest thing at first was sounding natural. I prepared too much and read every sentence from paper, so I sounded rather stiff. [21]A trainer told me to write only key words and speak as if I was talking to one friend[/21]. That advice helped immediately. Radio can feel strange because you cannot see listeners, but imagining one person makes your voice warmer. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy most about it now? || Guest: My programme includes local music, interviews and information about events. The part I like most is interviewing ordinary people with interesting stories. [22]I enjoy guests who are nervous at first but relax when they realise the interview is just a conversation[/22]. Often they leave saying it was easier than they expected. That makes me feel I have done my job well. || Interviewer: Have you changed the way you work since then? || Guest: Live radio has surprises. Once, the computer stopped playing music in the middle of the show. I could not fix it quickly, so [23]I talked about local events until a colleague solved the technical problem[/23]. It felt like a very long three minutes. Listeners probably thought it was planned, but my hands were shaking under the desk. || Interviewer: Is there anything that people misunderstand about this kind of work? || Guest: Community radio is different from national radio because people know you. [24]Listeners often send messages about tiny local details, like a closed road or a lost dog[/24]. Those messages may seem small, but they are useful. We can share them quickly, and someone nearby may hear and help. That is why the station matters to the town. || Interviewer: And what are your plans for the future? || Guest: In the future, I want more young people to join us. [25]We are setting up a weekend club where teenagers can learn to record short reports[/25]. They might interview sports teams, review films or talk about school projects. I hope some will later make their own programmes. Their voices would bring fresh energy to the station. || Interviewer: Finally, what advice would you give to someone who wants to try something similar? || Guest: I would say, start with a small step and do not wait until everything is perfect. When people begin something like community radio programme, they often worry too much about equipment, confidence or making mistakes. It is better to ask questions, watch people who already know the work, and practise a little at a time. You learn more from real situations than from planning for months. Also, be patient with yourself, because the first few attempts are usually slow, and that is completely normal. || Interviewer: Thank you, that was really interesting.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today we're talking to Sam Foster, who presents a community radio programme. Sam, you present a programme on community radio. How did you first get involved?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I used to listen to the station while cooking, but I never imagined speaking on it. One day they asked for volunteers to help at a local music day. I started by carrying equipment at an outdoor event, and the presenters noticed I was good at talking to visitors. A few weeks later they invited me to read short announcements, and slowly I became more confident behind the microphone.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was difficult when you were starting out?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: The hardest thing at first was sounding natural. I prepared too much and read every sentence from paper, so I sounded rather stiff. A trainer told me to write only key words and speak as if I was talking to one friend. That advice helped immediately. Radio can feel strange because you cannot see listeners, but imagining one person makes your voice warmer.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy most about it now?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: My programme includes local music, interviews and information about events. The part I like most is interviewing ordinary people with interesting stories. I enjoy guests who are nervous at first but relax when they realise the interview is just a conversation. Often they leave saying it was easier than they expected. That makes me feel I have done my job well.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Have you changed the way you work since then?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Live radio has surprises. Once, the computer stopped playing music in the middle of the show. I could not fix it quickly, so I talked about local events until a colleague solved the technical problem. It felt like a very long three minutes. Listeners probably thought it was planned, but my hands were shaking under the desk.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Is there anything that people misunderstand about this kind of work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Community radio is different from national radio because people know you. Listeners often send messages about tiny local details, like a closed road or a lost dog. Those messages may seem small, but they are useful. We can share them quickly, and someone nearby may hear and help. That is why the station matters to the town.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: And what are your plans for the future?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: In the future, I want more young people to join us. We are setting up a weekend club where teenagers can learn to record short reports. They might interview sports teams, review films or talk about school projects. I hope some will later make their own programmes. Their voices would bring fresh energy to the station.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what advice would you give to someone who wants to try something similar?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I would say, start with a small step and do not wait until everything is perfect. When people begin something like community radio programme, they often worry too much about equipment, confidence or making mistakes. It is better to ask questions, watch people who already know the work, and practise a little at a time. You learn more from real situations than from planning for months. Also, be patient with yourself, because the first few attempts are usually slow, and that is completely normal.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Thank you, that was really interesting.

questions.question: How did Sam first help the radio station?

options.A: He carried equipment at an event.
options.C: He played music on a programme.

questions.explanation: Sam says he started by carrying equipment at an outdoor event.

questions.question: What advice helped Sam sound more natural?

options.B: to use key words and imagine one listener

questions.explanation: A trainer told Sam to write only key words and speak as if he was talking to one friend.

questions.question: What does Sam enjoy about interviewing guests?

options.A: They usually bring music with them.
options.C: They become more relaxed during the conversation.

questions.explanation: Sam enjoys guests who are nervous at first but relax during the interview.

questions.question: What did Sam do when the computer stopped working?

options.A: He filled the time by talking about events.
options.C: He asked listeners to phone the station.

questions.explanation: Sam talked about local events until a colleague solved the technical problem.

questions.question: What kind of messages do listeners send?
questions.explanation: Sam says listeners send messages about tiny local details, like a closed road or a lost dog.

questions.question: What does Sam want teenagers to do?
questions.explanation: Sam says they are setting up a weekend club where teenagers can learn to record short reports.
